text:
a
We revisit the problem of realizing rotary propeller motion
of a devil-stick using continuous-time forcing. This problem
represents a nonprehensile manipulation task since the devil-
stick is manipulated without grasping, and is subject  to a
unilateral constraint at its point of contact with the manip-
ulator. Among others, nonprehensile manipulation has the
advantage of being able to control more degrees-of-freedom
(DOFs) than that of the manipulator [1], [2]. Nonprehensile
manipulation tasks may involve either continuous or intermit-
tent contact [2], [3] between the object and manipulator. The
work in this paper belongs to the former class of problems.
This work was supported by the National Science Foundation, under
Grant CMMI-2043464
The  authors  are with  the Department  of Mechanical  Engineer-
ing,  Michigan  State  University,  East  Lansing,  MI  48824,  USA
khande10@egr.msu.edu, mukherji@egr.msu.edu

Stick using Normal Forcing
n Mukherjee, Senior Member
Propeller motion of  a devil-stick has been previously
studied in [4], [5], [6], [7], [8]. In these works,  the devil-
stick was manipulated by controlling both the normal and
tangential forces applied on the devil-stick by the actuator.
In these works, it was assumed that the actuator rolls on the
devil-stick without slipping, which causes the contact point
to change over time. This further necessitates the assumption
that  the contact point instantaneously jumps to its original
value every time the devil-stick completes a rotation,  i.e.,
for every evolution of the orientation angle of the devil-stick
by 2œÄ rad. This resetting of  the contact point makes the
resulting propeller motion hybrid.
In this paper, we propose a different approach to realizing
stable propeller motion of a devil-stick.  In comparison to
existing work, the novelty of our approach is as follows:
‚Ä¢ The control inputs are chosen to be the normal force
applied on the devil-stick, and its location of applica-
tion.
‚Ä¢ There is no tangential force applied on the devil-stick
by the actuator.
‚Ä¢ For a stable propeller motion,  the control  inputs vary
smoothly, i.e.,  there is no instantaneous change in the
point of application of the force for every rotation of the
devil-stick. The resulting system is therefore not hybrid.
Virtual holonomic constraints (VHCs) [9], [10], [11] are
used to specify a circular trajectory for the center-of-mass of
the devil-stick. A continuous feedback linearizing controller
enforces the VHCs. A variation of  the impulse controlled
Poincare¬¥ map (ICPM) approach [12] is used to stabilize the
orbit corresponding to a desired propeller motion by varying
the applied normal force when the orientation of the devil-
stick crosses a certain value. Simulations demonstrate that the
presented approach can stabilize a desired propeller motion
from arbitrary initial conditions.

A. System Description
Consider the motion of a 3DOF rigid devil-stick in the
vertical xy plane using as shown in Fig. 1. The conÔ¨Åguration
of the devil-stick is speciÔ¨Åed by the three generalized coor-
dinates: (hx, hy, Œ∏), where hx and hy denote the Cartesian
coordinates of the center-of-mass G of the devil-stick, and
Œ∏ denotes the angle it makes with respect to the horizontal
x axis, measured positive counterclockwise. The devil-stick
is manipulated by means of a continuous force F applied
normal to the devil-stick at a distance r fromG. It is assumed
that  the manipulator applying the force can slide on the
devil-stick without friction. Since the number of available

which indicates stable, periodic behavior, consistent with the
results reported in [4],  [5]. The constraint manifold C is
comprised of a dense set of orbits which are stable, but not
asymptotically stable [10], [12]. The orbits are characterized
by the level sets of E(q2, qÀô2) in (31). Propeller motion is
achieved if the values of q2 and qÀô2 on C ensure that the value
of E is greater than the maximum value Pmax of P(q2), i.e.,
E > g/R, which corresponds to an orbit for which qÀô2 does
not change sign [10], [12].
For values of œÜ satisfying (18) chosen such that cot œÜ 6= 0,

have an Euler-Lagrange structure. Further,  it can be seen
from (29) that the system does not permit a closed, periodic
orbit as the magnitude of qÀô2 changes over every rotation as
q2 increments by 2œÄ, because of the term exp{‚àí2q2 cot œÜ}.

Remark 6: For the choice of orbit in (47), gain matrices in
(45) and initial conditions (50), the value of F > 0 ‚àÄ t since
the condition in Remark 3 is met, and the value of I(k) >
0 ‚àÄ k = 1, 2, . . . , 6. The approach presented here does not
explicitly constrain the control inputs, and the requirement
that F does not change sign may be violated if the initial
conditions require I(k) to be negative. This problem may be
addressed by using a control strategy such as MPC which
imposes input constraints.

A novel method for realizing rotary propeller motion of
a devil-stick is presented.  In contrast  to previous works,
which control the normal and tangential force applied to the
devil-stick, our approach controls the normal force and its
point of application on the devil-stick. The resulting propeller
motion in our approach is then such that the contact point
between the actuator and devil-stick is not reset after every
rotation of  the devil-stick. Virtual  holonomic constraints
are used to describe a family of stable propeller motions,

each with different speeds of rotation. A variation of  the
impulse controlled Poincare¬¥ map approach, wherein intermit-
tent impulsive forces are applied to the devil-stick without
loss of contact, is employed to stabilize a propeller motion
with a desired speed of rotation. As the system trajectory
converges to the desired propeller motion,  the magnitude
of  the impulsive forces converge to zero, and stability of
the trajectory is guaranteed under continuous control alone.
Simulation results demonstrate the efÔ¨Åcacy of the proposed
approach in stabilizing propeller motion from arbitrary initial
conditions. Future work will focus on control design with
explicit input constraints, and experimental validation.
